operations.rationale: Remove leading and trailing whitespace from time strings to ensure consistency in formatting.

Rare time values are corrected to the most frequent or semantically plausible canonical form based on value counts and context. For example, '12:00 a.m.' is corrected to '12:00 p.m.' as '12:00 a.m.' appears only once and is likely a typo; '10:39 a.m.' is corrected to '10:40 a.m.' due to high frequency of '10:40 a.m.' and proximity. Other values are mapped to nearest valid time entries in the distribution.
Remove leading, trailing, and multiple internal whitespaces to standardize formatting across titles.

Map all suspect values to canonical language codes based on frequency and candidate evidence. Prioritize 'eng' for English variants, 'jpn' for Japanese, 'ger' for German, etc. Rare or misspelled values (e.g., 'Koran' → 'kor', 'Germagno' → 'ger', 'Duut' → 'dut') are corrected to standard ISO codes. Multi-language entries are collapsed to primary language (e.g., 'engfre' → 'eng').
Standardize spacing by removing extra whitespaces and ensuring consistent formatting.

mapping.Journal of the American Dietetic Associa: Journal of the American Geriatrics Society
mapping.Journal of personality and social psycho: Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology
mapping.Journal of cancer research and clinical: Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology

operations.rationale: Map known journal titles to their canonical forms based on value counts and candidate mappings. Correct casing, spacing, and known mismatches (e.g., 'PloS one' → 'PLoS One', 'European journal of radiology' → 'European Journal of Anaesthesiology').

The column contains inconsistent casing (e.g., 'PloS one', 'PLoS ONE'). Standardizing to lowercase or title case improves consistency and enables accurate matching. Based on the value_counts, 'PloS one' is the dominant form, so we will standardize all entries to lowercase with a consistent format.

mapping.The American journal of managed care: The American journal of clinical nutrition
mapping.Journal of medical toxicology : official: Journal of orthopaedic science : official journal of the Japanese Orthopaedic Association
mapping.The European journal of general practice: The American journal of clinical nutrition
mapping.The Journal of hospital infection: The American journal of clinical nutrition

operations.rationale: Several rare values in the column are known variants or misspellings of established journal names. These should be mapped to their canonical forms based on the provided candidates to ensure consistency and accuracy in downstream analysis.

The column contains entries like '1932-6203 (Print) 0022-0345' and 'Feb-65', which are invalid or malformed. We will remove all non-numeric, non-hyphen characters and extract only the valid ISSN format (e.g., '1932-6203'). Entries like 'Feb-65' are invalid and should be flagged or removed.
Some entries may be missing or malformed. We will detect and handle entries that do not match the standard ISSN format (e.g., 'Feb-65') as invalid and flag them for review.
The column contains mostly valid numeric values. We will parse the values to ensure they are properly interpreted as integers. Missing values will be retained as null.
The column contains mostly valid numeric values. We will parse the values to ensure they are properly interpreted as integers. Missing values will be retained as null.

The column has 87 missing values, and while no explicit disguised nulls are detected, the format '1/1/YYYY' suggests that entries with missing or invalid dates may be represented as empty or malformed strings. This operation will ensure missing values are properly marked as null.
All values are already in lowercase or consistent format; no case standardization is needed.

The values are in a consistent 'M/D/YYYY' or '1/1/YYYY' format, but some are malformed (e.g., '4/2/15'). This operation will parse the date strings into a standard date format (YYYY-MM-DD), with invalid or malformed entries flagged for review.
The column contains leading/trailing whitespace in some entries, which may affect parsing or matching. Stripping whitespace ensures consistent formatting.
Some empty strings or whitespace-only entries may represent missing data. These will be converted to null values to maintain data integrity.

Several rare values in the pagination column are variants or typos of known valid entries. These are mapped to their canonical forms based on frequency and context. This ensures consistency in pagination ranges for downstream analysis.
Some entries contain leading or trailing whitespace that may interfere with parsing. Stripping ensures clean, consistent formatting.
Author names are already in consistent capitalization; no case standardization is required.
The value '{NULL}' appears 63 times and likely represents missing author data. These will be converted to null to maintain data integrity.

flags.rationale: Entries like 'Feb-65' do not conform to ISSN format (a valid ISSN is a string of 8 characters with hyphens, e.g., '1932-6203'). These should be flagged for manual review as they are not valid ISSN identifiers.

Values like '938', '4537', '1042', and '7462' are extremely high and inconsistent with typical journal issue numbers (which usually range from 0 to 12 or 20). These are likely errors and should be flagged for review.
While most values are in a reasonable range, values like '137' and '119' are unusually high for typical journal volumes. These should be flagged for review to ensure they are not data entry errors.

operations.rationale: Remove leading and trailing whitespace from all name entries to ensure consistency.
Map rare or misspelled restaurant names to their canonical equivalents based on value frequency and semantic similarity in the column's value_counts and suspect_values.
Remove leading and trailing whitespace to standardize address formatting.

Ensure consistent capitalization (e.g., '9th Ave' instead of '9th Ave' or '9TH AVE') by standardizing to title case or consistent format; however, given the lack of clear canonical casing in the data, only whitespace and unit removal are applied.
Map address variants to their most frequent or canonical counterparts based on value_counts and suspect_values, especially for known mismatches like Hollywood Blvd and 9th Ave.
Standardize city names to title case to ensure consistency with the dominant form in value_counts (e.g., 'Los Angeles' vs 'los angeles').

Rare zipCode values are variants of common ZIP codes. Based on value frequency and candidate mappings, these values are corrected to the most common canonical form (e.g., 10003, 19107, 85032) to maintain data consistency and accuracy.
Phone numbers are consistently formatted with parentheses and hyphens, but no punctuation inconsistencies are detected. No changes needed.

Website URLs with misspellings or non-standard names are mapped to the most common canonical domain based on value frequency and known business references. This ensures consistency and enables accurate downstream analysis.

Remove inconsistent punctuation (e.g., semicolons, extra slashes, trailing punctuation) and standardize URL formatting to a consistent base form (e.g., remove trailing slashes, convert semicolon-separated values to single entries).

The column contains string representations of years in business. All values are numeric strings, and the distribution shows consistent integer values. Parsing to int will standardize the data type and allow for numerical operations.
The column contains entries with leading/trailing or internal whitespace. Stripping whitespace ensures consistent formatting and prevents parsing errors when splitting phone/fax entries.
Some entries are empty or contain only whitespace, which may represent missing data. Normalizing these to null (or empty string) ensures consistency with missing value handling.

Several rare phone/fax entries are variants of known entries. Mapping them to the most common or canonical form (based on value counts and candidate lists) ensures consistency and improves data quality. These mappings are derived from the suspect_values and their candidates, with preference for higher-frequency or more common entries.
Whitespace at the beginning or end of entries can cause parsing or matching issues. Stripping ensures clean, consistent text.

Entries like 'Taco Bell Corp' and 'Taco Bell Corp.' or 'Tehran Restaurant' may be inconsistent in capitalization. Standardizing to title case or proper case improves consistency and matching accuracy.

Multiple suspect values in 'aka' are known to be misspellings or alternate names for established businesses. Mapping them to canonical names ensures consistency and enables accurate entity matching and downstream analysis.
